## reference · ux icon usability labels
* Icons must communicate meaning clearly to users; if not, they cause frustration and hinder task completion.
* Only a few icons have universal recognition, such as home, print, and magnifying glass for search.
* Most icons are ambiguous due to varying meanings across interfaces; examples include heart, star, and hamburger icons.
* A text label must be present alongside an icon to clarify its meaning and reduce ambiguity.
* Icon labels should be visible at all times, without interaction from the user (e.g., no hover).
* Icons are easily recognizable on mobile devices due to less competing content, but less noticeable on desktop screens.
* Relative size of an icon compared to other visual elements affects its noticeability; larger icons are more salient.
* When designing icons, do research on existing icons, keep designs simple and schematic, and test for recognizability and memorability.
* Always include a visible text label with an icon; as Bruce Tognazzini said, "a word is worth a thousand pictures."

Sources: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/icon-usability/
